title: Shipping production CV in six weeks: what to cut.
dek: My first deployment at Twyn went live six weeks after onboarding. The work wasn’t adding features — it was negotiating which ones not to ship.

children.text: The defect-detection system that now runs on a live automotive line at 97–98% accuracy didn’t start that way. It started as a scoping conversation about what could be cut without breaking the product. Six weeks isn’t enough time to build the system you want; it’s enough time to build the smallest system that can survive a shop floor.

The model wasn’t the bottleneck. The bottleneck was data — specifically, the labelling budget. We didn’t have time to build a perfectly balanced dataset across every defect class on the line. So we picked the three classes that mattered most to the quality team, set a higher confidence threshold for everything else, and routed the long-tail to human review.
Defect classes that occurred fewer than 20 times per shift.
Any feature that required new hardware on the line.
Any feature that required operators to change their workflow.
Anything that didn’t map cleanly to an action a supervisor could take.
Inference latency. Operator trust. Explainability. The boring parts — the parts a recruiter would never ask about — are the ones that decide whether the system is still running six months later.
Six weeks isn’t a build budget. It’s a discipline.
